{"file_path": "_src_ofac-application.md", "content": "# APPLICATION FOR SPECIFIC LICENSE

**Office of Foreign Assets Control**
**Licensing Division**
**U.S. Department of the Treasury**
**1500 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20220**

**Sanctions Program:** Syrian Sanctions Regulations, 31 C.F.R. Part 542; Executive Order 13582 of August 17, 2011

**Date of Application:** July 1, 2024

---

## I. Applicant Information

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Applicant / Licensee** | Meridian Biotech Solutions, Inc. |
| **Entity Type** | Delaware C-Corporation |
| **Principal Place of Business** | 4200 Lakeshore Boulevard, Suite 800, Cambridge, MA 02142 |
| **Employer Identification Number (EIN)** | 47-3928104 |
| **DUNS Number** | 08-471-3920 |
| **Chief Executive Officer** | Dr. Priya Ramaswamy |
| **General Counsel / Point of Contact** | Jonathan D. Halsted, (617) 555-0193, jhalsted@meridianbiotech.com |
| **Chief Compliance Officer** | Margaret "Peggy" Dunleavy |
| **Nature of Business** | Development and manufacture of in vitro diagnostic reagent kits, laboratory calibration instruments, and point-of-care testing platforms |

Meridian Biotech Solutions, Inc. ("Meridian" or the "Applicant") respectfully applies to the Office of Foreign Assets Control ("OFAC") for a specific license authorizing the export of cancer diagnostic reagent kits, calibration instruments, and related remote training services to Damascus Central University Hospital ("DCUH") in Damascus, Syria. The proposed transaction would otherwise be prohibited under the Syrian Sanctions Regulations, 31 C.F.R. Part 542, and Executive Order 13582 of August 17, 2011, because the end-user is an instrumentality of the Government of Syria and the destination is the Syrian Arab Republic.

## II. Authorized Representative / Outside Counsel

| Field | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Firm** | Ashford & Whitmore LLP |
| **Address** | 1700 K Street NW, Suite 950, Washington, DC 20006 |
| **Lead Partner** | Catherine R. Bellingham, Esq. |
| **Senior Associate** | David Osei-Mensah, Esq. |
| **Role** | Authorized representative for purposes of this application and all communications with OFAC |

All correspondence, questions, and licensing determinations concerning this application should be directed to Ashford & Whitmore LLP as authorized representative, with copies to Jonathan D. Halsted, General Counsel of Meridian.

## III. Requested Authorization and Effective Period

**Requested Authorization.** The Applicant requests a specific license authorizing, to the extent otherwise prohibited, the following transaction: the exportation and re-exportation from the United States to DCUH, Damascus, Syria, of (i) 500 CancerDetect RX-700 Reagent Kits; (ii) 4 CalibPro 3100 Calibration Units; and (iii) approximately 40 hours of remote installation and calibration training services delivered via secure video link from the Applicant's Cambridge, Massachusetts facility, together with all incidental transactions necessary to effect the shipment, customs clearance, and payment described in this application.

**Aggregate Transaction Value:** USD $1,280,000.

**Requested Effective Date:** September 1, 2024.

**Requested Expiration Date:** February 28, 2026 (an 18-month license term).

The Applicant has reviewed the general license provisions of the Syrian Sanctions Regulations, 31 C.F.R. Part 542, and has determined that no existing general license authorizes a commercial sale of medical diagnostic goods and services by a U.S. manufacturer to a Syrian governmental hospital. The available humanitarian general licenses are limited to donations and nongovernmental organization activities and do not extend to a commercial transaction of this kind. A specific license is therefore required.

## IV. Description of the Proposed Transaction

The Applicant proposes to export cancer diagnostic supplies to DCUH, the largest remaining oncology referral center in Syria. The transaction comprises three line items, described in detail in Section VI below and in the purchase order issued by DCUH (PO-DCUH-2024-0743, dated June 17, 2024; Exhibit A):

1. **CancerDetect RX-700 Reagent Kit** — 500 kits at $2,340 per kit ($1,170,000). In vitro diagnostic reagent kits for immunohistochemical staining of breast cancer biomarkers (HER2, ER, and PR).
2. **CalibPro 3100 Calibration Unit** — 4 units at $18,750 per unit ($75,000). Benchtop calibration instruments required to validate the reagent kits before clinical use.
3. **Remote Installation & Calibration Training** — 40 hours of live instruction over 8 weeks, flat fee of $35,000, delivered via secure video link from the Applicant's Cambridge, Massachusetts facility. No Meridian personnel will travel to Syria or to any intermediary country.

All physical goods are manufactured at the Applicant's Cambridge, Massachusetts facility under ISO 13485-certified quality management systems. Both physical products are classified EAR99 under the Commerce Control List. EAR99 classification does not exempt the goods from the Syrian sanctions program; OFAC authorization is required because the destination is Syria and the end-user is an instrumentality of the Government of Syria.

## V. Parties to the Transaction

The Applicant has conducted comprehensive sanctions and restricted-party screening of all transaction parties, their directors, principals, officers, and—where available through publicly accessible corporate registries and commercial databases—their beneficial owners. Screening was completed on June 3, 2024, against the OFAC Specially Designated Nationals and Blocked Persons List ("SDN List"), the OFAC Consolidated Sanctions List, the OFAC Sectoral Sanctions Identifications List, the BIS Entity List, the BIS Denied Persons List, the BIS Unverified List, and the United Nations Consolidated Sanctions List. The complete screening memorandum is attached as Exhibit B.

| Party | Role | Address | Principal(s) Screened | Screening Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Meridian Biotech Solutions, Inc. | Applicant / Exporter | 4200 Lakeshore Blvd, Ste 800, Cambridge, MA 02142 | Dr. Priya Ramaswamy (CEO); Jonathan D. Halsted (GC); Margaret Dunleavy (CCO) | No Match |
| Damascus Central University Hospital (DCUH) | End-User | Al-Mazzeh Highway, Bldg 7, Damascus, Syria | Dr. Faisal Kareem Al-Masri (Hospital Director) | No Match (governmental instrumentality; see §VII) |
| Syrian Ministry of Health | Governmental authority over DCUH | Damascus, Syria | N/A | No independent listing (governmental instrumentality) |
| Al-Rashid Medical Procurement Co. (ARMPC) | Customs clearance / import logistics agent | 18 Barada Street, Floor 3, Damascus, Syria | Tariq Nabil Hammoud (Managing Director) | No entity match; no match on Hammoud; **30% shareholder Samir Daoud Khoury is SDN-listed — disclosed in §IX** |
| Ankara Medical Transit Warehouse LLC (AMTW) | Turkish intermediary warehouse / re-export logistics | Organize Sanayi Bölgesi, No. 42, Ankara, Turkey | Elif Yılmaz (Managing Director) | No Match |
| Pinnacle Freight International, Inc. | U.S. freight forwarder | 9100 Port Commerce Drive, Newark, NJ 07114 | Lisa Marchetti (VP International Logistics) | No Match |
| Harborview National Bank | Applicant's U.S. receiving bank | Boston, MA | N/A | No Match |
| Remitting bank (to be confirmed) | DCUH's remitting bank | To be identified and screened | N/A | Must be non-SDN; Central Bank of Calverley excluded — see §IX |

The Applicant will re-screen all transaction parties at 90-day intervals during the pendency of this application and immediately prior to any shipment if the license is granted.

## VI. Goods and Services — Description and Export Classification

### 6.1 CancerDetect RX-700 Reagent Kit

| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Product** | CancerDetect RX-700 Reagent Kit |
| **Type** | In vitro diagnostic (IVD) reagent kit for immunohistochemical (IHC) staining of formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded tissue biopsies |
| **Intended Use** | Identification and semi-quantitative assessment of breast cancer biomarkers HER2, ER, and PR |
| **Regulatory Status** | FDA 510(k) cleared (K213847) |
| **Compatible Platform** | Ventana BenchMark XT automated staining system (the platform installed at DCUH) |
| **Quantity** | 500 kits |
| **Unit Price** | $2,340 |
| **Line Total** | $1,170,000 |
| **ECCN** | EAR99 |
| **HTS Code** | 3822.19.5000 |
| **Shelf Life** | 18 months from manufacture |
| **Storage / Handling** | 2–8°C refrigerated; cold-chain required throughout transit; ~1.8 kg per kit; ~1,500 kg total shipment weight |

The CancerDetect RX-700 is the only FDA-cleared reagent kit validated for use with the Ventana BenchMark XT tissue processing system already installed at DCUH. No substitute product is available that has been validated for that specific platform. The kit does not contain controlled technology or components and presents no dual-use concerns.

### 6.2 CalibPro 3100 Calibration Unit

| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Product** | CalibPro 3100 Calibration Unit |
| **Type** | Benchtop laboratory calibration instrument for validation of IHC reagent kit performance prior to clinical use |
| **Quantity** | 4 units |
| **Unit Price** | $18,750 |
| **Line Total** | $75,000 |
| **ECCN** | EAR99 |
| **HTS Code** | 9027.80.4530 |
| **Power Supply** | 220V / 50 Hz AC (compatible with Syrian and Turkish electrical grids) |
| **Weight / Dimensions** | 23 kg per unit (92 kg total); 45 × 38 × 30 cm |
| **Connectivity** | USB 2.0 port for data export only; no wireless or network connectivity |
| **Embedded Firmware** | Version 4.2.1; factory-installed; not user-modifiable |
| **Encryption** | AES-128 used solely for internal data-integrity verification of calibration results; below EAR de minimis thresholds for encryption items; does not alter EAR99 classification |

The CalibPro 3100 is a required companion device used to validate each new lot of CancerDetect RX-700 reagents before clinical deployment. The embedded firmware is integral to the instrument's operation and is not separately exportable. The Applicant confirms that no firmware updates, patches, or modifications beyond the factory-installed version 4.2.1 are requested under this license; any future firmware update would constitute a separate transaction requiring independent authorization.

### 6.3 Remote Installation and Calibration Training Services

| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Service** | Remote installation guidance, calibration verification, and operator training |
| **Delivery Method** | Secure video conferencing link from Meridian's Cambridge, MA facility; no personnel travel to Syria or any intermediary country |
| **Duration** | 40 hours of live instruction over 8 weeks (~5 hours/week) |
| **Fee** | $35,000 (flat) |
| **Delivered By** | Meridian Field Applications Science team (2–3 application scientists and 1 calibration engineer) |
| **Participants** | 2–6 DCUH laboratory personnel |

The training curriculum comprises six modules: (1) system unpacking and physical setup; (2) instrument schematics review; (3) software diagnostic interface training; (4) reagent kit calibration protocol; (5) clinical workflow integration; and (6) ongoing maintenance and troubleshooting. The technical information provided is limited to the operation, calibration, and maintenance of EAR99-classified equipment necessary for its safe and effective clinical use. The training does not involve the transfer of design, development, or production technology, and no controlled technology is exported. The provision of these services to persons in Syria requires OFAC authorization and is expressly included within the scope of the requested license.

## VII. End-User and End-Use

**End-User.** Damascus Central University Hospital, Al-Mazzeh Highway, Building 7, Damascus, Syrian Arab Republic, Attention: Dr. Faisal Kareem Al-Masri, Hospital Director. DCUH is a public teaching hospital affiliated with the University of Damascus, Faculty of Medicine, and operates under the administrative authority and budgetary oversight of the Syrian Ministry of Health. DCUH is not independently listed on the SDN List or any other restricted-party list. As a public hospital, DCUH is an instrumentality of the Government of Syria, which is comprehensively sanctioned under Executive Order 13582 and 31 C.F.R. Part 542. Specific OFAC authorization is accordingly required for this transaction.

**End-Use.** The goods and services are intended exclusively for clinical diagnostic use within the Oncology Department of DCUH. The CancerDetect RX-700 kits will be used for immunohistochemical staining of tissue biopsy samples to identify breast cancer biomarkers (HER2, ER, PR) in patients referred to DCUH. The CalibPro 3100 units will validate and calibrate the reagent kits for use with the Ventana BenchMark XT platform installed in the DCUH Oncology Laboratory. The training will enable DCUH laboratory personnel to install, calibrate, and operate the equipment in accordance with manufacturer specifications.

DCUH processes approximately 3,800 tissue biopsies per year. The requested quantity of 500 reagent kits is anticipated to meet the hospital's diagnostic needs for approximately 15 months. DCUH has confirmed in its purchase order that the goods will not be re-exported, diverted, or transferred to any third party without the prior written consent of the Applicant and in compliance with all applicable laws, and that the goods will remain at all times within the custody and control of DCUH and be used solely for the clinical diagnostic purposes described above.

**End-Use Monitoring.** The Applicant will implement a written end-use monitoring plan (Exhibit H) that includes: (a) delivery confirmation with documentary and photographic evidence; (b) an end-user certificate and non-diversion commitment executed by DCUH; (c) periodic usage reporting at no less than annual intervals during the license term; and (d) a mechanism for site verification, whether remote or in-person, as feasible. The Applicant will report any indication of diversion, re-export, or unauthorized transfer to OFAC promptly.

## VIII. Shipping Route and Logistics

The Applicant has engaged Pinnacle Freight International, Inc. ("Pinnacle"), a U.S. freight forwarder (no sanctions matches), to coordinate physical transportation. The complete logistics plan is attached as Exhibit D. The proposed five-leg route is as follows:

| Leg | Route Segment | Mode | Estimated Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cambridge, MA → Port Newark, NJ | Refrigerated truck (domestic) | 1–2 days |
| 2 | Port Newark, NJ → Mersin Port, Turkey | Ocean freight (reefer container, 4°C ± 2°C) | 18–22 days |
| 3 | Mersin Port → Ankara, Turkey | Refrigerated truck; warehousing at AMTW (5–10 days) | 7–12 days |
| 4 | Ankara → Bab al-Hawa border crossing | Refrigerated truck (re-export from Turkey) | 2–3 days |
| 5 | Bab al-Hawa → Damascus, Syria | Overland; Syrian customs clearance and final delivery | 2–3 days |
| | **Total estimated transit** | | **~4–6 weeks (plan for 6–8 weeks)** |

**Incoterms:** DAP — Damascus, Syria (Incoterms 2020).

**Cold-Chain Management.** The CancerDetect RX-700 kits require continuous maintenance at 2–8°C throughout transit. All kits will be shipped in validated insulated containers with calibrated digital temperature data loggers recording at 15-minute intervals. The reefer container for the ocean leg will be set to 4°C ± 2°C with remote performance monitoring. The AMTW warehouse in Ankara maintains dedicated pharmaceutical-grade cold storage verified to sustain the 2–8°C range. Any temperature excursion exceeding 8°C or falling below 2°C for more than 60 consecutive minutes will trigger immediate notification and a product-integrity assessment. All temperature data logs will be compiled and provided to the Applicant for quality assurance and end-user documentation.

**Intermediaries.** AMTW (Ankara, Turkey; no sanctions matches) serves as the intermediate warehousing and re-export facility. ARMPC (Damascus, Syria) serves as the in-country customs clearance and import logistics agent; the sanctions issue associated with ARMPC is addressed in Section IX. No other intermediaries are involved.

## IX. Disclosure of Sanctions-Related Issues and Proposed Mitigation

The Applicant makes the following disclosures in the interest of complete transparency, consistent with the obligation to provide OFAC with a full and accurate statement of all material facts.

### 9.1 SDN-Listed Shareholder of the Customs Clearance Agent (ARMPC / Samir Daoud Khoury)

ARMPC, the customs clearance and import logistics agent designated by DCUH, is not itself listed on the SDN List or any other restricted-party list, and its Managing Director, Tariq Nabil Hammoud, returned no matches. However, through the Applicant's enhanced beneficial-ownership screening platform (implemented January 2024), the Applicant identified that **Samir Daoud Khoury** holds a 30% ownership interest in ARMPC. Mr. Khoury is designated on the SDN List. The listing details are:

> **KHOURY, Samir Daoud** — DOB: March 12, 1971; Nationality: Syria; Passport No.: S-0048712; SDN List Addition Date: April 15, 2024; Basis for Designation: Acting on behalf of a sanctioned Syrian military procurement network.

**50 Percent Rule Analysis.** Under OFAC's 50 Percent Rule (31 C.F.R. § 501.501), an entity is considered blocked only where SDN-listed persons own, individually or in the aggregate, 50% or more of the entity. Mr. Khoury's 30% ownership stake falls below this threshold, and ARMPC is therefore not automatically considered a blocked entity. The Applicant nevertheless discloses this ownership interest because OFAC guidance warns that transacting with entities in which SDN-listed persons hold any ownership interest may present elevated sanctions risk, and because complete disclosure is required.

**Proposed Mitigation.** The Applicant proposes the following measures to address this issue:

1. **Substitution of the Customs Clearance Agent (Primary Approach).** The Applicant is actively identifying an alternative Syrian customs clearance and import logistics agent that has no SDN-listed ownership or principals. Upon identification, the Applicant will submit the replacement agent's identifying information to OFAC for review and screening confirmation prior to any shipment, and will amend the transaction structure accordingly. The Applicant requests that OFAC authorize the transaction on the basis that ARMPC will be replaced by a vetted, sanctions-clean agent before shipment.

2. **Limited-Role Authorization (Alternative Approach).** In the event that a suitable alternative agent cannot be identified in time, the Applicant requests, in the alternative, that OFAC authorize ARMPC's participation solely in the limited capacity of in-country customs clearance performed on behalf of DCUH, subject to the following conditions: (a) ARMPC's role is strictly limited to customs clearance and in-country delivery coordination; (b) no U.S.-origin funds, and no portion of the licensed transaction value, will pass to ARMPC or to Mr. Khoury; (c) all customs fees and charges payable to ARMPC will be paid by DCUH from funds that do not originate from any SDN-listed institution; (d) the Applicant will have no direct or indirect dealings with Mr. Khoury; and (e) the Applicant will re-screen ARMPC and its ownership immediately prior to shipment and will report any change to OFAC.

The Applicant will accept any condition OFAC imposes with respect to ARMPC, including a requirement that ARMPC be removed from the transaction structure entirely.

### 9.2 SDN-Listed Remitting Bank Identified in the Purchase Order (Central Bank of Calverley)

The DCUH purchase order (Exhibit A) specifies that payment be made by wire transfer from DCUH's account at the **Central Bank of Calverley (CBS)**, Damascus. CBS is listed on the SDN List (added August 10, 2011, pursuant to E.O. 13582). Payment routed through CBS would constitute a prohibited transaction with a blocked person, and any such funds received by a U.S. financial institution would be subject to blocking.

**Proposed Mitigation.** The Applicant will not accept or process any payment routed through CBS or any other SDN-listed institution. The Applicant is working with DCUH to amend the payment terms of the purchase order to provide for remittance through a non-SDN-listed financial institution. The Applicant requests that the license authorize payment by wire transfer from a non-SDN-listed financial institution (either a non-SDN Syrian commercial bank or a third-country bank) to the Applicant's account at Harborview National Bank, Boston, Massachusetts (Account No. 7821-4490-3361). The specific remitting institution will be identified, screened against all applicable sanctions lists, and confirmed to OFAC (if required) prior to any payment. This approach mirrors the payment condition imposed under the Applicant's prior specific license (SYR-2021-384712), which expressly prohibited routing payment through CBS or any SDN-listed institution.

### 9.3 Governmental End-User

As stated in Section VII, DCUH is an instrumentality of the Government of Syria and is comprehensively sanctioned under E.O. 13582 and 31 C.F.R. Part 542. The Applicant requests specific authorization to transact with DCUH for the limited humanitarian purpose described in this application. The Applicant is not aware of any SDN designation of DCUH, its director, or its oncology department personnel.

## X. Humanitarian Justification

The proposed transaction serves a compelling humanitarian purpose that is well documented by independent international assessment.

The World Health Organization Syria Country Office published a Comprehensive Health Needs Assessment in January 2024 (Exhibit E) that identifies cancer diagnostics as a **critical gap** in the Syrian health system and names DCUH specifically as the largest and most important remaining oncology referral center in Syria. Key findings include:

- Cancer incidence in Syria is estimated at 22,000–25,000 new cases per year. Breast cancer is the most prevalent malignancy among Syrian women, accounting for approximately 33% of all female cancer diagnoses.
- Of approximately 14 immunohistochemistry laboratories functioning in Syria prior to 2011, only three remain partially operational, and none at full pre-conflict capacity.
- DCUH operates a 140-bed oncology ward and processes approximately 3,800 tissue biopsies per year, bearing the substantial majority of the country's cancer diagnostic workload.
- The diagnostic backlog at DCUH exceeds 14 months, forcing oncologists to initiate empiric chemotherapy without biomarker confirmation in many cases, resulting in suboptimal treatment, avoidable drug toxicity, and inefficient use of scarce pharmaceutical supplies.
- The estimated five-year survival rate for breast cancer in Syria has declined from approximately 62% prior to 2011 to approximately 38% at present, attributable in significant part to prolonged diagnostic delays and the inability to perform biomarker-guided treatment selection.
- WHO specifically recommends urgent procurement of validated IHC reagent kits (with priority for HER2/ER/PR breast cancer panels), replacement of laboratory calibration instruments, remote technical training, and international coordination to facilitate humanitarian licensing for the import of essential medical diagnostic supplies.

The CancerDetect RX-700 is the only FDA-cleared reagent kit validated for the Ventana BenchMark XT platform already installed at DCUH, meaning there is no substitute product that would satisfy the hospital's diagnostic need. The CalibPro 3100 units are required to validate the reagent kits before clinical use; DCUH's existing calibration equipment is reported to be non-functional or beyond serviceable life. The requested 500 kits will meet approximately 15 months of DCUH's diagnostic demand.

The WHO assessment expressly calls on the international community to ensure that sanctions frameworks do not impede the delivery of essential medical diagnostic supplies to the Syrian population, consistent with humanitarian principles and applicable exemptions. The Applicant submits that this transaction directly advances that humanitarian objective.

The Applicant previously held OFAC Specific License No. **SYR-2021-384712**, issued September 14, 2021, and expired March 31, 2023 (Exhibit F). That license authorized the export of tuberculosis (TB) in vitro diagnostic reagent kits (TBDetect Pro-200, 200 kits, total value $340,000) to Al-Mujtahid Hospital, Damascus, Syria—a public hospital affiliated with the Syrian Ministry of Health—for humanitarian medical use.

All authorized transactions under that license were completed by January 2023, and all reporting conditions were satisfied in a timely manner. The Applicant has no record of OFAC violations, civil monetary penalties, cautionary or warning letters, findings of violation, or voluntary self-disclosures. The prior license demonstrates the Applicant's demonstrated capacity to administer OFAC-licensed transactions in a comprehensively sanctioned jurisdiction, including compliance with conditions prohibiting payment through CBS or any SDN-listed institution and requiring post-shipment reporting.

## XII. Applicant's OFAC Compliance Program

The Applicant maintains an OFAC sanctions compliance program that has been in place since 2015 and was most recently independently audited by Graystone Compliance Partners LLC in September 2023 (Exhibit G). The audit assigned the program an overall rating of "Satisfactory with Recommendations," reflecting that the program meets the core expectations set forth in OFAC's "A Framework for OFAC Compliance Commitments" (May 2019). - **Written Policies and Procedures.** A comprehensive Sanctions Compliance Manual (last updated March 2023) covering OFAC, BIS/EAR, and anti-boycott requirements, including the export of physical goods and the provision of remote services, with clear escalation procedures.

In January 2024, the Applicant implemented enhanced real-time beneficial-ownership screening that automatically re-screens equity holders of counterparties whenever sanctions lists are updated—the enhancement that identified the ownership interest described in Section 9.1.
- **Training.** Annual OFAC and sanctions compliance training completed by 97% of relevant personnel in fiscal year 2023, with targeted supplemental training for international sales, logistics, and finance teams.
- **Record-Keeping.** Transaction files maintained for a minimum of five years in accordance with 31 C.F.R. § 501.601, with electronic backup and indexing for rapid retrieval.
- **No Enforcement History.** No OFAC violations, civil monetary penalties, cautionary letters, warning letters, findings of violation, or voluntary self-disclosures.

The Applicant respectfully proposes, and will accept, the following conditions, consistent with the conditions imposed under the Applicant's prior specific license:

1. The license authorizes only the specific transaction described in this application. No other transactions with Syria or the Government of Syria are authorized.
2. The authorized goods shall be delivered solely to DCUH and shall not be diverted, re-exported, or transferred to any other party or end-user within Syria or any other country without prior written authorization from OFAC.
3. No transaction authorized under the license shall involve any person on the SDN List, or any entity in which an SDN-listed person holds a 50% or greater ownership interest. The Applicant will re-screen all parties immediately prior to shipment.
4. Payment shall be effected only through a non-SDN-listed financial institution and shall not be routed through the Central Bank of Calverley or any other SDN-listed institution.
5. The Applicant shall maintain complete records of all transactions conducted pursuant to the license for not less than five (5) years from the date of the last transaction, in accordance with 31 C.F.R. § 501.601.

**50 Percent Rule Analysis.** Under OFAC's 50 Percent Rule (31 C.F.R. § 501.501), an entity is considered blocked only where SDN-listed persons own, individually or in the aggregate, 50% or more of the entity. Mr. Khoury's 30% ownership stake falls below this threshold, and ARMPC is therefore not automatically considered a blocked entity. The Applicant nevertheless discloses this ownership interest because OFAC guidance warns that transacting with entities in which SDN-listed persons hold any ownership interest may present elevated sanctions risk, and because complete disclosure is required.

**Proposed Mitigation.** The Applicant proposes the following measures to address this issue:

1. **Substitution of the Customs Clearance Agent (Primary Approach).** The Applicant is actively identifying an alternative Syrian customs clearance and import logistics agent that has no SDN-listed ownership or principals. The Applicant requests that OFAC authorize the transaction on the basis that ARMPC will be replaced by a vetted, sanctions-clean agent before shipment.

The Applicant will accept any condition OFAC imposes with respect to ARMPC, including a requirement that ARMPC be removed from the transaction structure entirely.

## XI. Prior OFAC License History

The Applicant previously held OFAC Specific License No. **SYR-2021-384712**, issued September 14, 2021, and expired March 31, 2023 (Exhibit F). That license authorized the export of tuberculosis (TB) in vitro diagnostic reagent kits (TBDetect Pro-200, 200 kits, total value $340,000) to Al-Mujtahid Hospital, Damascus, Syria—a public hospital affiliated with the Syrian Ministry of Health—for humanitarian medical use.

All authorized transactions under that license were completed by January 2023, and all reporting conditions were satisfied in a timely manner. The Applicant has no record of OFAC violations, civil monetary penalties, cautionary or warning letters, findings of violation, or voluntary self-disclosures. The prior license demonstrates the Applicant's demonstrated capacity to administer OFAC-licensed transactions in a comprehensively sanctioned jurisdiction, including compliance with conditions prohibiting payment through CBS or any SDN-listed institution and requiring post-shipment reporting.

## XII. Applicant's OFAC Compliance Program

The Applicant maintains an OFAC sanctions compliance program that has been in place since 2015 and was most recently independently audited by Graystone Compliance Partners LLC in September 2023 (Exhibit G). The audit assigned the program an overall rating of "Satisfactory with Recommendations," reflecting that the program meets the core expectations set forth in OFAC's "A Framework for OFAC Compliance Commitments" (May 2019). Program features include:

- **Management Commitment.** Visible executive support from the CEO and General Counsel; the Chief Compliance Officer reports to the General Counsel and maintains a standing quarterly briefing to the Board's Audit Committee.
- **Written Policies and Procedures.** A comprehensive Sanctions Compliance Manual (last updated March 2023) covering OFAC, BIS/EAR, and anti-boycott requirements, including the export of physical goods and the provision of remote services, with clear escalation procedures.
- **Screening Protocols.** Automated screening of all counterparties, end-users, intermediaries, freight forwarders, and financial institutions against the SDN List, the Consolidated Screening List, EU sanctions lists, and UN sanctions lists, conducted at transaction initiation and at each material milestone. In January 2024, the Applicant implemented enhanced real-time beneficial-ownership screening that automatically re-screens equity holders of counterparties whenever sanctions lists are updated—the enhancement that identified the ownership interest described in Section 9.1.
- **Training.** Annual OFAC and sanctions compliance training completed by 97% of relevant personnel in fiscal year 2023, with targeted supplemental training for international sales, logistics, and finance teams.
- **Record-Keeping.** Transaction files maintained for a minimum of five years in accordance with 31 C.F.R. § 501.601, with electronic backup and indexing for rapid retrieval.
- **No Enforcement History.** No OFAC violations, civil monetary penalties, cautionary letters, warning letters, findings of violation, or voluntary self-disclosures.

## XIII. Proposed License Conditions

The Applicant respectfully proposes, and will accept, the following conditions, consistent with the conditions imposed under the Applicant's prior specific license:

1. The license authorizes only the specific transaction described in this application. No other transactions with Syria or the Government of Syria are authorized.
2. The authorized goods shall be delivered solely to DCUH and shall not be diverted, re-exported, or transferred to any other party or end-user within Syria or any other country without prior written authorization from OFAC.
3. No transaction authorized under the license shall involve any person on the SDN List, or any entity in which an SDN-listed person holds a 50% or greater ownership interest. The Applicant will re-screen all parties immediately prior to shipment.
4. Payment shall be effected only through a non-SDN-listed financial institution and shall not be routed through the Central Bank of Calverley or any other SDN-listed institution.
5. The Applicant shall maintain complete records of all transactions conducted pursuant to the license for not less than five (5) years from the date of the last transaction, in accordance with 31 C.F.R. § 501.601.
6. The Applicant shall submit a written report to OFAC within thirty (30) days of completion of each shipment, including proof of delivery, copies of shipping documents, and confirmation of end-user receipt, together with temperature data logs confirming cold-chain integrity.
7. The Applicant shall implement the end-use monitoring plan described in Section VII and Exhibit H and shall report any indication of diversion promptly.
8. The license does not authorize any firmware update, patch, or modification to the CalibPro 3100 beyond the factory-installed version 4.2.1; any such update shall constitute a separate transaction requiring independent authorization.
9. The license does not relieve the Applicant of any obligations under the Export Administration Regulations, 15 C.F.R. Parts 730–774, administered by BIS.
10. Any material change in the facts or circumstances described in this application—including any change in the customs clearance agent, the remitting bank, or the ownership of any transaction party—shall be reported to OFAC promptly and may require amendment or reissuance of the license.

## XIV. Supporting Documentation (Exhibits)

| Exhibit | Document |
|---|---|
| A | DCUH Purchase Order No. PO-DCUH-2024-0743 (June 17, 2024) |
| B | Meridian Compliance Screening Memorandum (June 10, 2024) |
| C | Product Technical Data Sheets — CancerDetect RX-700 and CalibPro 3100 (TDS-2024-0347, Rev. March 15, 2024) |

| G | Graystone Compliance Partners LLC, OFAC Compliance Program Audit Executive Summary (Sept. 29, 2023) |
| H | Meridian End-Use Monitoring Plan (to be submitted) |
| I | Consolidated Party Screening Matrix |

## XV. Certification and Signature

The Applicant certifies that the foregoing application is true, complete, and accurate to the best of its knowledge and belief, and that it will comply with all conditions of any license issued pursuant to this application. The Applicant understands that any false, incomplete, or misleading statement may result in denial of the application, revocation of any license issued, and the imposition of civil or criminal penalties.

Respectfully submitted,

**MERIDIAN BIOTECH SOLUTIONS, INC.**

By: \_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

Ashford & Whitmore LLP

{"content": "# PRIVILEGED ATTORNEY WORK PRODUCT — ATTORNEY-CLIENT COMMUNICATION

## ISSUES MEMORANDUM

**TO:** Jonathan D. Halsted, General Counsel, Meridian Biotech Solutions, Inc.
**CC:** Margaret "Peggy" Dunleavy, Chief Compliance Officer, Meridian Biotech Solutions, Inc.; Engagement File
**FROM:** Catherine R. Bellingham, Partner; David Osei-Mensah, Senior Associate — Ashford & Whitmore LLP
**DATE:** June 28, 2024
**RE:** Legal Issues and Recommended Strategy — Proposed OFAC Specific License Application for the Export of CancerDetect RX-700 Reagent Kits, CalibPro 3100 Calibration Units, and Remote Training Services to Damascus Central University Hospital, Damascus, Syria

**PRIVILEGE NOTE.** This memorandum is prepared by outside counsel in anticipation of preparing and filing an OFAC specific license application on behalf of Meridian Biotech Solutions, Inc. ("Meridian" or the "Company"). It constitutes attorney work product and a confidential attorney-client communication and is intended solely for the named recipients. It should not be distributed outside the addressees without the prior written consent of Ashford & Whitmore LLP.

---

## I. Introduction and Executive Summary

You have asked us to identify and analyze the legal issues presented by Meridian's proposed export of cancer diagnostic products and related remote training services to Damascus Central University Hospital ("DCUH") in Damascus, Syria, and to recommend a strategy for the specific license application we will file with the U.S. Department of the Treasury, Office of Foreign Assets Control ("OFAC"). This memorandum is based on our review of the documents Meridian has provided, including the client transaction summary email of June 24, 2024; the Compliance Screening Memorandum prepared by Peggy Dunleavy dated June 10, 2024; DCUH Purchase Order No. PO-DCUH-2024-0743 dated June 17, 2024; the combined product technical data sheets (TDS-2024-0347); the Pinnacle Freight International logistics plan dated June 25, 2024; the WHO Syria Country Office needs assessment (January 2024); the prior OFAC Specific License SYR-2021-384712; and the Graystone Compliance Partners audit summary dated September 29, 2023.

**Bottom line.** The transaction is a strong candidate for a specific license: the goods are humanitarian medical diagnostics, the end-use is well documented by the WHO, the products are EAR99, and Meridian has a clean compliance record and a successful prior OFAC license for a materially similar transaction. Two issues, however, are material and must be resolved before filing or addressed squarely within the application:

1. **An SDN-listed individual (Samir Daoud Khoury) owns 30% of the Syrian customs clearance agent (ARMPC).** ARMPC is not blocked under OFAC's 50 Percent Rule, but the SDN nexus must be fully disclosed and the transaction structure should be adjusted—ideally by substituting a sanctions-clean agent—before OFAC will be comfortable authorizing the transaction.

2. **The purchase order routes payment through the Central Bank of Calverley, an SDN-listed bank.** This payment mechanism is prohibited and cannot be used. The payment terms must be restructured to route through a non-SDN-listed financial institution before the transaction can proceed, and the application must reflect the restructured payment flow.

In addition, the remote training services raise technology-transfer and "services-to-Syria" issues that must be scoped carefully, and the in-country delivery leg (handled by ARMPC, through the Bab al-Hawa crossing into a conflict zone) raises diversion and end-use monitoring concerns that we should address proactively in the application. We recommend filing promptly, but only after the payment restructuring is underway and a decision is made on the ARMPC substitution. The client's target effective date of September 1, 2024 is aggressive given OFAC processing times of 90–180 days and should be managed as a target, not a commitment.

## II. Factual Background

Meridian proposes to export to DCUH: (i) 500 CancerDetect RX-700 Reagent Kits ($1,170,000); (ii) 4 CalibPro 3100 Calibration Units ($75,000); and (iii) approximately 40 hours of remote installation and calibration training ($35,000)—an aggregate transaction value of $1,280,000. DCUH is a public teaching hospital under the Syrian Ministry of Health and an instrumentality of the Government of Syria. The goods are EAR99 but require OFAC authorization because the destination is Syria and the end-user is a governmental instrumentality. The goods will ship from Cambridge, Massachusetts, via Port Newark to Mersin Port, Turkey, then overland through Ankara (warehoused at Ankara Medical Transit Warehouse LLC) and the Bab al-Hawa border crossing to Damascus. Payment under the purchase order is stated as a 100% advance wire from DCUH's account at the Central Bank of Calverley to Meridian's account at Harborview National Bank. Meridian held a prior specific license (SYR-2021-384712) for a comparable humanitarian export to a Syrian public hospital, which it completed without incident.

## III. Legal Framework

- **Executive Order 13582 (August 17, 2011)** blocks the Government of Syria and prohibits the exportation, re-exportation, sale, or supply of any goods or services to Syria, and any transaction dealing in the property of the Government of Syria.
- **The Syrian Sanctions Regulations, 31 C.F.R. Part 542**, implement E.O. 13582. Because DCUH is an instrumentality of the Government of Syria, any transaction with DCUH is prohibited absent OFAC authorization.
- **OFAC's 50 Percent Rule (31 C.F.R. § 501.501)** provides that an entity is blocked if one or more SDN-listed persons own, individually or in the aggregate, 50% or more of the entity. Below that threshold, the entity is not automatically blocked, but OFAC guidance warns that dealings with entities in which SDN-listed persons hold any ownership interest present elevated risk and warrant caution and disclosure.
- **The Export Administration Regulations (EAR), 15 C.F.R. Parts 730–774**, administered by the Bureau of Industry and Security ("BIS"), classify both physical products as EAR99. EAR99 classification does not exempt the goods from OFAC's Syria prohibitions. The EAR also independently control the export of "technology" and "technical data," which is relevant to the training services.
- **Recordkeeping (31 C.F.R. § 501.601)** requires licensees to retain records of licensed transactions for at least five years.

## IV. Issues and Analysis

### Issue 1 — A specific license is required; no general license covers this transaction.

**Analysis.** DCUH is an instrumentality of the Government of Syria and the destination is Syria, so the transaction is prohibited under E.O. 13582 and 31 C.F.R. Part 542 absent authorization. We have reviewed the general license provisions of Part 542. The available humanitarian general licenses are limited to donations and nongovernmental organization activities and do not authorize a commercial sale of goods and services by a U.S. manufacturer to a Syrian governmental hospital. A specific license is therefore required. This is consistent with the path Meridian took for the prior, comparable transaction.

**Recommendation.** Proceed with a specific license application. Lead with the humanitarian purpose and the WHO documentation, and cite the prior license as precedent. Confirm during drafting that no general license (including any recently issued humanitarian authorizations) has been expanded to cover commercial medical sales to governmental end-users.

### Issue 2 — SDN-listed 30% shareholder of the customs clearance agent (ARMPC / Samir Daoud Khoury).

**Analysis.** This is the most significant issue. ARMPC is not on the SDN List, and its managing director screened clean, but Meridian's enhanced beneficial-ownership screening identified that Samir Daoud Khoury—a person designated on the SDN List on April 15, 2024, for acting on behalf of a sanctioned Syrian military procurement network—owns 30% of ARMPC. Under the 50 Percent Rule, ARMPC is not automatically blocked because Khoury's stake is below 50%. However, OFAC treats SDN involvement at any ownership level as a serious red flag, and the designation basis (a Syrian military procurement network) is particularly adverse in the context of a transaction whose end-user is a Syrian governmental hospital. Failing to disclose this would be a material omission; disclosing it without a mitigation plan would likely slow or jeopardize review.

The structural problem is that ARMPC sits on the in-country delivery leg—the point at which the goods are most vulnerable to diversion and at which a sanctioned actor could theoretically exercise influence. Even if Khoury has no operational role, his ownership creates both a compliance risk and a perception risk.

**Options.**

- **(a) Substitute ARMPC with a sanctions-clean agent (preferred).** Have DCUH designate an alternative Syrian customs clearance and import logistics agent with no SDN-listed ownership or principals. We would screen the replacement and submit its details to OFAC for review before shipment. This is the cleanest path and materially improves the odds and speed of approval. The downside is identifying a qualified, screened alternative inside Syria on the client's timeline.
- **(b) Request authorization for ARMPC in a strictly limited customs-clearance role, with conditions.** If substitution is not feasible, we can request that OFAC authorize ARMPC's participation limited to in-country customs clearance on behalf of DCUH, on conditions that (i) no U.S.-origin funds or any portion of the transaction value pass to ARMPC or to Khoury; (ii) all customs charges are paid by DCUH from non-SDN funds; (iii) Meridian has no dealings with Khoury; and (iv) Meridian re-screens ARMPC's ownership immediately before shipment and reports any change. This is defensible but riskier; OFAC may still require substitution.
- **(c) Eliminate the in-country agent entirely.** Not realistic—someone must clear Syrian customs and arrange final delivery. This is not a viable option but is noted for completeness.

**Recommendation.** Pursue option (a) as the primary strategy and option (b) as a fallback. In the application, disclose the Khoury ownership fully, present the 50 Percent Rule analysis, state that Meridian is substituting ARMPC with a vetted agent (to be submitted to OFAC before shipment), and—in the alternative—request limited-role authorization with the conditions above if substitution proves infeasible. Begin working with Meridian and DCUH immediately to identify a replacement agent, because this is the item most likely to drive the timeline.

### Issue 3 — SDN-listed remitting bank (Central Bank of Calverley).

**Analysis.** The purchase order routes payment through the Central Bank of Calverley ("CBS"), which has been on the SDN List since August 10, 2011 (designated under E.O. 13582). Processing a wire from CBS would be a prohibited transaction with a blocked person, and any funds received would be subject to blocking. Notably, Meridian's prior license expressly prohibited routing payment through CBS or any SDN-listed institution and authorized payment only through a non-SDN-listed Syrian commercial bank—so OFAC has already spoken to this exact issue in Meridian's favor.

**Recommendation.** Do not accept or process any payment through CBS. Restructure the payment terms in the purchase order before filing (or at minimum before any payment) so that DCUH remits through a non-SDN-listed financial institution—either a non-SDN Syrian commercial bank or a third-country bank—to Meridian's account at Harborview National Bank. In the application, request authorization for payment via a non-SDN-listed institution, identify the specific institution once screened, and expressly exclude CBS and any SDN-listed institution. Cite the prior license's identical payment condition as precedent. Peggy's team should screen any proposed replacement bank before we name it in the application.

### Issue 4 — Remote training services: "services to Syria" and technology-transfer scope.

**Analysis.** Two distinct concerns arise from the 40-hour remote training engagement.

First, **the provision of services to Syria is independently prohibited** under E.O. 13582 and 31 C.F.R. Part 542, separate from the export of physical goods. The training must therefore be expressly within the scope of the requested license; it is not incidental to the goods export as a matter of law.

Second, **the training transfers technical information.** Modules 2 and 3 are the sensitive ones: Module 2 walks through proprietary instrument schematics and engineering diagrams, and Module 3 provides the 287-page CalibPro 3100 Software Reference Manual, firmware diagnostic logs, and calibration algorithm parameters. Even though the products are EAR99, the "technology" and "technical data" shared during training are subject to the EAR and must be characterized carefully. If the training crosses from operation/maintenance information into design or development technology, BIS classification questions arise. The datasheet appropriately notes that any firmware update beyond v4.2.1 is a separate transaction requiring independent authorization, and that post-training technical support involving additional technical data may require separate authorization—both correct and helpful.

**Recommendation.** Include the training services expressly within the license request. In the application, characterize the technical information as limited to the operation, calibration, and maintenance of EAR99 equipment necessary for safe clinical use, and state that no design, development, or production technology is transferred and no controlled technology is exported. Confirm with Meridian's engineering and regulatory teams that the schematics and Software Reference Manual do not contain technology that would warrant a higher ECCN than EAR99 (this overlaps with Issue 6). Build into the license conditions that no firmware updates or post-training technical support involving additional technical data are authorized under the license without separate authorization.

### Issue 5 — End-use monitoring and diversion risk.

**Analysis.** The in-country leg is the highest diversion risk: goods cross at Bab al-Hawa into a conflict zone and are handled by ARMPC (which has the SDN ownership issue) for customs clearance and final delivery to DCUH. The Graystone audit (Finding 2) flagged that Meridian's post-delivery end-use monitoring for the prior license was limited to confirming delivery receipt, with no ongoing verification that goods remained with the end-user or were used for the stated purpose. OFAC expects robust end-use monitoring for specific licenses in comprehensively sanctioned jurisdictions, and a weak monitoring plan is a potential basis for conditions or denial.

**Recommendation.** Include a written end-use monitoring plan as an exhibit to the application. The plan should provide for: (a) delivery confirmation with documentary and photographic evidence; (b) an end-user certificate and non-diversion commitment executed by DCUH; (c) periodic usage reporting at no less than annual intervals; and (d) a site-verification mechanism (remote or in-person) as feasible. Tie the plan to the cold-chain data logs, which already create a documentary trail. Commit to prompt reporting of any diversion indication. A credible monitoring plan both strengthens the application and implements Graystone's recommendation.

### Issue 6 — Encryption / firmware classification confirmation.

**Analysis.** The CalibPro 3100 contains embedded firmware (v4.2.1) that uses AES-128 encryption for internal data-integrity verification. The datasheet asserts that the encryption functionality falls below EAR de minimis thresholds for encryption items and does not alter the EAR99 classification. Encryption items can be a trap: even limited encryption functionality can implicate EAR Category 5, Part 2 and the associated classification, reporting, and § 740.17 license-exception requirements. Graystone's audit (Finding 3) specifically recommended that Meridian's risk assessments document embedded software, firmware, and encryption classification for new products.

**Recommendation.** Before filing, obtain written confirmation from Meridian's engineering and regulatory teams (and, if there is any doubt, a BIS self-classification or advisory opinion) that the AES-128 functionality is properly classified EAR99 and that no separate BIS authorization is required for the export of the units with embedded firmware. Document the analysis in the application file. This both satisfies Graystone's recommendation and preempts a likely OFAC/BIS question. Reaffirm in the application that no firmware update is requested under the license.

### Issue 7 — Re-screening and material-change obligations.

**Analysis.** Sanctions lists change. Khoury was designated only on April 15, 2024—roughly seven weeks before Meridian's screening—illustrating how quickly a clean party can become problematic. The prior license imposed a condition requiring prompt reporting of any material change in facts or circumstances. If we substitute ARMPC or change the remitting bank after the license issues, those are material changes that must be reported and may require amendment.

**Recommendation.** Build 90-day re-screening of all parties into the application (Peggy has already recommended this), and commit to an immediate pre-shipment re-screen. Include a condition acknowledging the duty to report material changes—particularly any change in the customs clearance agent, the remitting bank, or the ownership of any party. Make clear in the application that the specific remitting bank and (if applicable) the replacement customs agent will be confirmed to OFAC before shipment.

### Issue 8 — Reporting and recordkeeping conditions.

**Analysis.** The prior license required a written report to OFAC within 30 days of each shipment, with proof of delivery, shipping documents, and end-user receipt, plus five-year recordkeeping under 31 C.F.R. § 501.601. We should expect, and welcome, similar conditions.

**Recommendation.** Propose these conditions in the application (30-day post-shipment reports including temperature data logs; five-year recordkeeping) so that OFAC sees Meridian is prepared to administer them. Meridian successfully satisfied these conditions under the prior license, which we should cite.

### Issue 9 — Timing and the September 1, 2024 target effective date.

**Analysis.** OFAC processing for specific license applications commonly runs 90–180 days, and complex applications with SDN-adjacent parties and payment-structuring issues tend toward the longer end. The client targets a September 1, 2024 effective date. If we file in early July, 90 days lands in early October. OFAC can issue licenses with effective dates that predate the issuance date, but the client should not rely on that.

**Recommendation.** File as soon as the payment restructuring is underway and the ARMPC decision is made—do not wait for every exhibit to be perfect, but do not file with the CBS payment mechanism or an undisclosed Khoury issue. Manage the September 1 date with the client as a target. Consider requesting a license term of September 1, 2024 through February 28, 2026 (18 months) to preserve runway for shipment and the 6–8 week transit, and to allow for re-shipment if cold-chain integrity is compromised.

### Issue 10 — Cold-chain feasibility (operational note, not a sanctions issue).

**Analysis.** The CancerDetect RX-700 kits require continuous 2–8°C storage across a 6–8 week transit through a conflict zone, with a 14-month diagnostic backlog at the destination. A temperature excursion above 12°C or below 0°C voids the warranty and may compromise reagent integrity. This is primarily an operational and quality matter, but it has licensing relevance: if goods arrive compromised and must be re-shipped, the re-shipment must occur within the license term and may require reporting.

**Recommendation.** Ensure the logistics plan's cold-chain protocol (validated containers, 15-minute data loggers, reefer container at 4°C ± 2°C, AMTW cold storage, excursion escalation) is reflected in the application, and build the license term and reporting conditions to accommodate a potential re-shipment. Confirm that DCUH has adequate refrigerated storage to receive and maintain the full shipment on arrival—Pinnacle has flagged this as an open item.

## V. Recommended Application Strategy

1. **Lead with the humanitarian case.** Open the application narrative with the WHO needs assessment, the breast cancer burden, the 14-month diagnostic backlog, the survival-rate decline, and the fact that the CancerDetect RX-700 is the only FDA-cleared kit validated for DCUH's installed platform. This is the strongest part of the matter.
2. **Cite the prior license as precedent.** Emphasize SYR-2021-384712, the clean completion, the satisfied reporting conditions, and the identical CBS-payment prohibition—OFAC has already trusted Meridian with a comparable transaction.
3. **Disclose the two SDN issues fully and present mitigation.** Address Khoury/ARMPC and CBS in a dedicated disclosure section. Present substitution of ARMPC as the primary path and limited-role authorization as the alternative; present the non-SDN payment restructuring as already underway. Transparency here is essential and is itself a compliance positive.
4. **Scope the training carefully.** Include the services in the license request and characterize the technical information as operation/maintenance-level only, with no controlled technology and no firmware updates authorized.
5. **Include an end-use monitoring plan and proposed conditions.** Show OFAC that Meridian will administer the license rigorously, including post-shipment reporting, re-screening, and material-change reporting.
6. **Document the compliance program.** Use the Graystone audit and the January 2024 beneficial-ownership enhancement (which is what caught the Khoury issue) to demonstrate a maturing, effective program.
7. **File promptly but cleanly.** Do not file until the payment restructuring is in motion and the ARMPC decision is made. Filing with the CBS payment mechanism or an undisclosed SDN nexus would be counterproductive.

## VI. Open Items and Next Steps

| # | Item | Owner | Needed For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify and screen a replacement Syrian customs clearance agent (preferred) | Meridian / DCUH / Peggy | Application filing (primary) or pre-shipment (if fallback used) |
| 2 | Amend PO payment terms to remove CBS; identify and screen a non-SDN remitting bank | Meridian / DCUH / Peggy | Application filing |
| 3 | Written confirmation of EAR99 classification of CalibPro 3100 firmware/encryption | Meridian engineering & regulatory | Application filing |
| 4 | Confirm training technical data is operation/maintenance-level only (no controlled technology) | Meridian regulatory | Application filing |
| 5 | Draft end-use monitoring plan | Ashford & Whitmore / Peggy | Application exhibit |
| 6 | Confirm DCUH refrigerated receiving capacity | Pinnacle / ARMPC (or replacement) / DCUH | Pre-shipment |
| 7 | Assemble exhibits A–I and finalize application | Ashford & Whitmore | Filing |
| 8 | Set client expectations on the September 1, 2024 target date | Ashford & Whitmore / Jon Halsted | Now |

## VII. Conclusion

This is a meritorious humanitarian export supported by independent WHO documentation, EAR99-classified products, a clean compliance record, and a successful prior OFAC license for a comparable transaction. The principal obstacles are the SDN-listed 30% shareholder of the Syrian customs clearance agent and the SDN-listed remitting bank named in the purchase order—both of which are addressable through transaction restructuring and full disclosure. We recommend proceeding to prepare the specific license application immediately, working in parallel on the ARMPC substitution and the payment restructuring, and filing as soon as those items are sufficiently advanced. We will circulate a draft application for your review before filing.

Please contact us with any questions.

**Ashford & Whitmore LLP**

Catherine R. Bellingham
Partner
1700 K Street NW, Suite 950, Washington, DC 20006

David Osei-Mensah
Senior Associate

*This memorandum is privileged and confidential. It does not constitute a guarantee of any licensing outcome, which rests with OFAC.*", "file_path": "_src_issues-memo.md"}
